items.q: What caused the French Revolution of 1789?
items.correct: Financial crisis from war debts, an unfair tax system burdening the Third Estate, Enlightenment ideas, and royal mismanagement of the Estates-General.
items.misconception: The starving poor stormed the Bastille and overthrew the king.
items.random_wrong: Napoleon led the revolution to replace the Bourbon monarchy.
items.why: Students see the revolution as a peasant uprising. In fact it was led by middle-class lawyers and professionals (the bourgeoisie); food riots were a symptom, not the engine.

items.q: Why did Caribbean migrants come to Britain in 1948 on the Windrush?
items.correct: Britain faced post-war labour shortages; the 1948 British Nationality Act gave Commonwealth citizens UK residency rights and Caribbean migrants answered the call to rebuild.
items.misconception: Windrush migrants were illegal immigrants from outside the empire.
items.random_wrong: Windrush migrants came as refugees from Caribbean famines.
items.why: They arrived as British subjects with full legal rights — the irony of the modern 'Windrush scandal'. Students miss the citizenship framework that made the migration unproblematic in law.

items.q: Did the Battle of Bosworth in 1485 end the Wars of the Roses?
items.correct: It killed Richard III and put Henry Tudor on the throne, but Yorkist threats (Simnel 1487, Warbeck 1490s) continued — the wars only truly ended with Stoke Field in 1487.
items.misconception: Bosworth in 1485 ended the Wars of the Roses immediately.
items.random_wrong: Bosworth was a naval battle fought off the south coast of England.
items.why: Henry VII faced rebellions for over a decade. Students learn 1485 as a tidy endpoint; in fact securing the dynasty took further fighting and ruthless control of pretenders.

items.q: What were Cromwell's disciplined cavalry called?
items.why: Correct answer: Ironsides. “Cavaliers” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which battle in 1645 was the decisive Parliament victory?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Naseby. “Marston Moor” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the Battle of Naseby?
items.why: Exact answer: 1645. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
